source.title: Chemical Reaction, Decomposition, and Toxic Gas Release at Bio-Lab, Inc.

event.top_event: Loss of Containment - Toxic Chlorine Gas Release
event.incident_type: Chemical decomposition and fire resulting in toxic gas release
actions_taken: Shelter-in-place order issued for nearby community
Louisiana State Police closed portion of Interstate 10 at 9:54 a.m.
Bio-Lab employees attempted to activate backup generator to power fire water pump
Bio-Lab employees manually started one diesel fire water pump at approximately 11:40 a.m.
Bio-Lab employees attempted to spray water on Plant 4 building
Bio-Lab employees identified and stopped water leak in fire water system at adjacent Lonza facility
US Fire Pump third-party response resource engaged
Facility completely reconstructed and resumed operations around March 2023
event.summary: On August 27, 2020, Category 4 Hurricane Laura caused severe damage to Bio-Lab's Lake Charles facility in Westlake, Louisiana. Extreme winds tore off portions of the roof of Plant 4, which stored approximately 70,000-100,000 pounds of TCCA-based formulation. Rainwater from the hurricane contacted the stored TCCA-based formulation, initiating a chemical reaction and decomposition that released toxic chlorine gas and caused a fire. The decomposition and fire destroyed Plant 4 and damaged additional structures. A massive plume of hazardous gases crossed the facility boundary and was visible over a large portion of the nearby community. Interstate 10 was closed for over 28 hours, and a shelter-in-place order was issued. No injuries were reported. Approximately $250 million was invested in redesigning and reconstructing the facility, which resumed operations around March 2023.
recommendations: Bio-Lab Lake Charles: Evaluate hazards from hurricanes and implement processes and safeguards for protection, including constructing buildings to withstand hurricane winds and flooding, implementing safeguards to ensure hazardous chemicals are not compromised during extreme weather, and following CCPS guidance on natural hazards assessment and planning
Bio-Lab Lake Charles: Develop and implement improved PHA action item management system with assignment, documentation, and tracking of all action items until resolution, and conduct periodic audits
Bio-Lab Lake Charles: Perform PHAs on all buildings and units processing or storing TCCA, revalidate at least every five years, and include building design basis as process safety information

Bio-Lab Lake Charles: Revise emergency response plan to require proper maintenance and function-testing of fire protection system, checking emergency equipment one month before hurricane season, and training personnel on emergency generators and equipment

Louisiana Governor and State Legislature: Require existing chemical manufacturing and storage facilities in hurricane-prone regions to evaluate hazards from hurricanes and implement protective processes and safeguards

OSHA: Amend PSM Standard to achieve more comprehensive control of reactive hazards, broaden application to cover reactive hazards from process-specific conditions and combinations of chemicals, and augment PHA element to explicitly require evaluation of reactive hazards
EPA: Revise Accidental Release Prevention Requirements to explicitly cover catastrophic reactive hazards from self-reactive chemicals and combinations of chemicals and process-specific conditions
EPA: Implement five open recommendations from 2022 GAO Report on chemical accident prevention related to climate change and natural hazards risks

consequences.name: Toxic chlorine gas release to atmosphere
Massive toxic gas plume crossing facility boundary

controls.name: Building design to withstand hurricane winds
controls.barrier_role: Prevent roof damage and water ingress
controls.lod_basis: Primary structural protection against extreme weather

supporting_text: extreme winds from Category 4 Hurricane Laura caused severe damage to buildings storing chemical formulations
winds from Hurricane Laura caused portions of the roof of Bio-Lab Plant 4 to be torn off the building

Bio-Lab's 2010 Process Hazard Analysis recommended the Lake Charles facility to 'consider evaluating warehouse roof structure for hurricane conditions; verify warehouse is built to withstand high winds'
The Bio-Lab Lake Charles facility, built in 1979, did not conform to the safeguards identified in the National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) 101 Life Safety Code

controls.name: Process Hazard Analysis (PHA) and action item management
controls.barrier_role: Identify and manage hazards through systematic analysis
controls.lod_basis: Proactive hazard identification and risk management

supporting_text: Bio-Lab's 2010 Process Hazard Analysis recommended the Lake Charles facility to 'consider evaluating warehouse roof structure for hurricane conditions; verify warehouse is built to withstand high winds,' but the company did not implement the recommendation
A lack of close-out documentation for the 2010 PHA recommendation indicates deficiencies in Bio-Lab's PHA action item management system
Bio-Lab voluntarily implemented some elements of the PSM standard, including periodically conducting Process Hazard Analyses (PHAs)

controls.name: Fire protection system with electric and diesel fire water pumps
controls.barrier_role: Suppress fire and control decomposition reaction
controls.lod_basis: Active fire suppression to mitigate consequences of chemical decomposition

supporting_text: Bio-Lab experienced serious delays in responding to the TCCA-based formulation decomposition and fire due to an inadequate and largely nonfunctional fire protection system

One of the employees attempted to turn on a rental backup generator secured during the site's preparation for Hurricane Laura to energize the large electric fire water pump. Without this generator, there was no way to operate this pump since the facility did not have electrical power
The employee, who was unfamiliar with the rental generator, was unable to start it

A wastewater treatment operator familiar with the fire protection equipment attempted to start the rental backup generator and energize the electric fire pump without success. He was, however, successful in manually starting one diesel fire water pump

By around 11:40 a.m., with the sole diesel fire water pump pressurizing the fire water system, the supervisor, two production operators, and the maintenance supervisor began turning on fire water monitors
The supervisor quickly realized there was inadequate water pressure at the monitor

Bio-Lab did not adequately maintain its fire protection system to ensure its functionality and did not ensure that enough of its staff, including its assigned hurricane crew, knew how to activate the rental backup generator

controls.name: Backup emergency generator
controls.barrier_role: Provide electrical power to fire protection equipment during power outage
controls.lod_basis: Support for critical fire suppression systems during loss of main power

supporting_text: Bio-Lab's backup emergency generator was not functional
A backup generator was required to provide electrical power to the electric pumps during a power outage
the facility did not have electrical power, and Bio-Lab's backup emergency generator was not functional

controls.name: Rental backup generator with operator training
controls.barrier_role: Provide temporary electrical power to fire protection equipment
controls.lod_basis: Contingency power supply for critical fire suppression systems

supporting_text: One of the employees attempted to turn on a rental backup generator secured during the site's preparation for Hurricane Laura to energize the large electric fire water pump
The employee, who was unfamiliar with the rental generator, was unable to start it
A wastewater treatment operator familiar with the fire protection equipment attempted to start the rental backup generator and energize the electric fire pump without success
Bio-Lab did not ensure that enough of its staff, including its assigned hurricane crew, knew how to activate the rental backup generator
Site personnel must be trained on the use of all emergency generators and other emergency equipment at least one month before the start of the U.S. hurricane season

controls.name: Automatic fire sprinkler system
controls.barrier_role: Automatically suppress fire and control decomposition reaction
controls.lod_basis: Automatic fire suppression independent of manual intervention

supporting_text: Bio-Lab did not conform to NFPA 400 Hazardous Materials Code, which required a fire detection system and an automatic fire sprinkler system
the absence of automated sprinkler systems

Had the Bio-Lab buildings involved in the incident been equipped with automatic extinguishing systems, it is likely that Bio-Lab would have begun applying large amounts of water to the decomposing TCCA-based formulation earlier in the incident

controls.name: Extreme weather preparation and planning
controls.barrier_role: Prevent or minimize exposure of hazardous materials to extreme weather
controls.lod_basis: Proactive preparation to prevent hazardous material exposure during hurricanes

supporting_text: Bio-Lab did not learn the importance of preparing for extreme weather after the 2017 Arkema incident in Crosby, Texas
Bio-Lab did not implement industry guidance for extreme weather preparation that was updated and published after the Arkema incident
as a result, was unprepared for the winds produced by Category 4 Hurricane Laura
two additional scheduled trucks never arrived at the facility, leaving over one million pounds of TCCA-based formulation product on-site as the hurricane approached

controls.name: Regulatory coverage under PSM and RMP standards
controls.barrier_role: Ensure baseline process safety management system elements are implemented
controls.lod_basis: Regulatory requirement for process safety management

supporting_text: TCCA and TCCA-based formulations are not covered by the Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) Process Safety Management (PSM) Standard
TCCA and TCCA-based formulation are not regulated under the OSHA PSM Standard or the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) Risk Management Program (RMP) Rule
Bio-Lab was not required to implement baseline process safety management system elements to manage the safety of its TCCA-based formulation related operations under these regulations
This regulatory coverage gap relating to reactive chemicals and their hazards contributed to this incident

controls.name: Compliance with NFPA 101 Life Safety Code
controls.barrier_role: Ensure automatic extinguishing systems or other protection to minimize danger
controls.lod_basis: Code-mandated fire protection for high-hazard industrial occupancies

supporting_text: The Bio-Lab Lake Charles facility, built in 1979, did not conform to the safeguards identified in the National Fire Protection Association (NFPA) 101 Life Safety Code for high-hazard industrial occupancies, which required automatic extinguishing systems or other protection to minimize danger to occupants before they have time to evacuate
Existing facilities in Louisiana, such as Bio-Lab, are grandfathered to the state-adopted codes and standards at the time of construction unless changes are made

controls.name: Fire protection system maintenance and function testing
controls.barrier_role: Ensure fire protection system is operational and ready for use
controls.lod_basis: Preventive maintenance to ensure barrier functionality

supporting_text: Bio-Lab experienced serious delays in responding to the TCCA-based formulation decomposition and fire due to an inadequate and largely nonfunctional fire protection system
Bio-Lab did not adequately maintain its fire protection system to ensure its functionality
The site's fire protection system is properly maintained and routinely function-tested in accordance with published industry guidance and NFPA requirements

controls.name: Elevated storage of TCCA-based formulation above floor level
controls.barrier_role: Prevent floodwater contact with stored chemicals
controls.lod_basis: Physical separation to prevent water contact during flooding

supporting_text: To protect the remaining TCCA-based formulation and other products from potential floodwater, the Hurricane Crew raised the materials above floor level by placing the material on triple-stacked pallets
winds from Hurricane Laura caused portions of the roof of Bio-Lab Plant 4 to be torn off the building
rainwater from Hurricane Laura had contacted the TCCA-based formulation material in Plant 4

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
